%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-plan" data-visual-plan-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
# Some Definitions and Properties

> **Section Objective:** Recognize special matrix types and basic matrix properties used throughout matrix algebra.

---

## Concepts In This Section

- diagonal matrix
- identity matrix
- zero matrix
- symmetric matrix
- matrix equality
- transpose

## 1. Diagonal matrix

$$\text{Diagonal matrix:}\quad A=(a_{ij})\text{ is square and }a_{ij}=0\text{ whenever }i\ne j.\[4pt]\begin{bmatrix}2&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&5\end{bmatrix}$$
*## 1. Diagonal matrix

A diagonal matrix must be **square** (same number of rows and columns), and every entry **off the main diagonal must equal zero**.

Here, \(a_{ij}\) denotes the entry in row \(i\) and column \(j\). When \(i = j\), the entry lies on the **main diagonal**. When \(i \ne j\), the entry is **off the main diagonal** and must be \(0\).

The example above is a \(3 \times 3\) diagonal matrix with diagonal entries \(2\), \(1\), and \(5\).

> **Exam Trigger:** Phrase — *"zero everywhere except on the main diagonal."*

#### Common Misuse
Diagonal entries do **not** have to equal \(1\), and they are even allowed to be \(0\). Only the off-diagonal entries are forced to be zero.*

## 2. Identity matrix

$$\text{Identity matrix:}\quad I_n=(\delta_{ij}),\qquad \delta_{ij}=\begin{cases}1,&i=j\\0,&i\ne j\end{cases}\[4pt]I_3=\begin{bmatrix}1&0&0\\0&1&0\\0&0&1\end{bmatrix}$$
*## 2. Identity matrix

The **identity matrix** (also called the **unit matrix**) is a special diagonal matrix with every main-diagonal entry equal to exactly \(1\) and every off-diagonal entry equal to \(0\).

\(I_n\) denotes the \(n \times n\) identity matrix. The subscript \(n\) is often omitted when the size is clear from context. The symbol \(\delta_{ij}\) is the Kronecker delta: it equals \(1\) when \(i = j\) and \(0\) otherwise.

The example \(I_3\) shown above is the \(3 \times 3\) identity matrix.

> **Exam Trigger:** Words like *"identity,"* *"unit matrix,"* or *"unity on the diagonal."*

#### Common Misuse
Any diagonal matrix is **not** automatically an identity matrix. Identity requires every diagonal entry to be exactly \(1\), not just any nonzero value.*

## 3. Zero matrix

$$\text{Zero matrix:}\quad 0_{m\times n}=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&\cdots&0\\0&0&\cdots&0\\\vdots&\vdots&\ddots&\vdots\\0&0&\cdots&0\end{bmatrix}$$
*## 3. Zero matrix

A **zero matrix** has every single entry equal to \(0\). The notation \(0_{m \times n}\) denotes the zero matrix with \(m\) rows and \(n\) columns.

**Concrete example** — a \(2 \times 3\) zero matrix:

$$
0_{2\times 3}=\begin{bmatrix}0&0&0\\0&0&0\end{bmatrix}
$$

Use this definition whenever an operation or condition produces no nonzero entries.

> **Exam Trigger:** Phrase — *"all elements are zero."*

#### Common Misuse
A zero matrix does **not** have to be square. It can be rectangular (e.g., \(2 \times 3\)) unless the problem explicitly adds a square-matrix condition.*

## 4. Symmetric matrix

$$\text{Symmetric matrix:}\quad A\text{ is square and }a_{ij}=a_{ji}\text{ for all }i,j.\quad \text{Equivalently, }A=A^T.\[4pt]\begin{bmatrix}4&-2&7\\-2&5&0\\7&0&1\end{bmatrix}$$
*## 4. Symmetric matrix

A **symmetric matrix** is square and **mirrors itself across the main diagonal**. The condition \(a_{ij} = a_{ji}\) means the entry in row \(i\), column \(j\) must equal the entry in row \(j\), column \(i\) — for every pair \((i, j)\).

In the example above, check one mirrored pair: the entry at position \((1,2)\) is \(-2\), and the entry at position \((2,1)\) is also \(-2\). Similarly, position \((1,3)\) and \((3,1)\) both hold \(7\), and positions \((2,3)\) and \((3,2)\) both hold \(0\).

An equivalent compact statement: \(A = A^T\).

> **Exam Trigger:** *"Symmetry about the main diagonal"* or *"Is \(A\) symmetric?"*

#### Common Misuse
Being **square is necessary but not sufficient**. You must also verify that every mirrored pair of off-diagonal entries matches.*

## 5. Equality of matrices

$$A=(a_{ij})_{m\times n},\quad B=(b_{ij})_{m\times n}.\[2pt]A=B\quad \Longleftrightarrow\quad a_{ij}=b_{ij}\text{ for every }i,j.$$
*## 5. Equality of matrices

Two matrices \(A\) and \(B\) are **equal** only when two conditions both hold:
1. They have the **same order** (same number of rows and same number of columns).
2. Every **corresponding entry** is equal: \(a_{ij} = b_{ij}\) for every \(i\) and \(j\).

**Concrete example:**

$$
\begin{bmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{bmatrix} \quad \text{but} \quad \begin{bmatrix}1&2\\3&4\end{bmatrix} \ne \begin{bmatrix}1&2\\4&3\end{bmatrix}
$$

The second pair fails because position \((2,1)\) holds \(3\) in the left matrix but \(4\) in the right.

> **Exam Trigger:** A problem asks you to solve for unknowns by setting two matrices equal.

#### Common Misuse
Containing the same set of numbers is **not** enough — positions must match exactly.*

## 6. Transpose

$$A=(a_{ij})_{m\times n}\quad \Longrightarrow\quad A^T=(a_{ji})_{n\times m},\qquad (A^T)^T=A.\[4pt]A=\begin{bmatrix}2&1&0\\3&-1&4\end{bmatrix}\quad \Longrightarrow\quad A^T=\begin{bmatrix}2&3\\1&-1\\0&4\end{bmatrix}$$
*## 6. Transpose

The **transpose** of \(A\), written \(A^T\), is formed by **interchanging rows and columns**: row \(i\) of \(A\) becomes column \(i\) of \(A^T\).

**Order change:** If \(A\) has \(m\) rows and \(n\) columns (order \(m \times n\)), then \(A^T\) has \(n\) rows and \(m\) columns (order \(n \times m\)).

In the example above, \(A\) is \(2 \times 3\). Row 1 of \(A\) is \([2, 1, 0]\), which becomes column 1 of \(A^T\). Row 2 of \(A\) is \([3, -1, 4]\), which becomes column 2 of \(A^T\). The result \(A^T\) is \(3 \times 2\).

Note also that \((A^T)^T = A\): transposing twice returns the original matrix.

> **Exam Trigger:** Notation \(A^T\) or instruction *"transpose the matrix."*

#### Common Misuse
Transpose **only changes positions**, never values. Do not negate or alter any entry.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-visual-meta" data-visual-kind="interactive_demo" data-teaching-role="concept_anchor" data-visual-use-b64="" style="display:none;"></div><div class="kc-interactive-demo" data-demo-b64=""></div>%%KC_END%%

---
**📌 Key Takeaways**
- Diagonal matrix: off-diagonal entries are all zero; the matrix must be square.
- Identity matrix: diagonal entries are all 1, off-diagonal entries are all 0.
- Zero matrix: every entry is zero; it need not be square.
- Symmetric matrix: \(A = A^T\); mirrored entries across the main diagonal match.
- Matrix equality: same order and every corresponding entry must match.
- Transpose: rows become columns; order changes from \(m \times n\) to \(n \times m\).

*Next, these definitions become tools for matrix algebra operations such as addition, scalar multiplication, and matrix multiplication.*

%%KC_BLOCK%%<div class="kc-quiz-plan" data-quiz-b64="" style="display:none;"></div>%%KC_END%%
